headline: GRAINS DRIFT, CATTLE HOLD, OIL EXTENDS SLIDE
subheadline: Quiet session as markets digest Iran deal hopes and planting progress.
lead: Corn closed $4.55¼, off a nickel in thin trade as the Belt caught a planting window between rain systems. Cattle held $239.30 while oil extended yesterday's Iran-premium deflation on diplomatic speculation, down another 2.1% to $90.15. Days like this are how the market builds the next move. Wait.
the_takeaway: Quiet days price the next move, not the noise.
teaser: Markets marking time as Iran premium deflates and Corn Belt stays on schedule.

one_number.context: Missouri and Iowa farmers caught consecutive dry days to push planting progress above the seasonal pace. The Belt is on schedule despite the wet start, removing weather premium from the July contract. No stress pricing until prevent plant deadlines approach in early June.

yesterdays_call.summary: Iran premium deflating as market prices escalation fatigue over extension risk.
yesterdays_call.note: Oil extended the slide another 2.1% on U.S.-Iran deal speculation.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time
sections.body: Corn closed $4.55, down a quarter-cent as **planting progress caught up to the calendar**. Missouri and Iowa farmers used consecutive dry days to push through delayed bean acres, with the Belt now running ahead of the 5-year average despite the wet May start. Soybeans gained a penny to $11.88 on meal strength, but the move lacks conviction. Wheat fell 7 cents to $6.26 on fund liquidation, no specific catalyst driving the selling. Range-bound isn't drama, but it's information: nobody has new news worth repositioning for.
sections.catalyst: Midwest planting window between rain systems pushes corn progress above seasonal pace
sections.bottom_line: Belt on schedule, no weather premium until prevent plant deadlines.

sections.title: Cattle Complex Steady
sections.body: Live cattle held $239.30, down a tenth in narrow trade as the complex **consolidated Tuesday's convergence**. Feeders matched at $349.38, the spread holding tight at 110 points after Monday's dramatic narrowing from 134. The fed cattle trade hasn't reset since last week's Cattle on Feed showed record numbers at 180+ days, but nobody's pressing the action until the cash market speaks. Box beef cutout unchanged, packers patient, feeders not panicking.
sections.bottom_line: Complex acting like the buyer is patient, not gone.

sections.title: Oil Extends Iran Slide
sections.body: WTI crude fell another 2.1% to $90.15 as **U.S.-Iran deal speculation gained momentum** after yesterday's 3.6% crash. Traders pricing escalation fatigue over extension risk as diplomatic channels reportedly opened through Swiss intermediaries. The Strait of Hormuz premium that added $8 to crude since March is deflating faster than it built. Natural gas gained 4 tenths to $3.01, but the move is domestic weather-driven, not geopolitical. Iran premium coming out of the energy complex systematically.
sections.catalyst: U.S.-Iran diplomatic progress speculation following Swiss channel reports
sections.bottom_line: Iran premium deflating systematically across energy complex.

outside_the_pit.title: Lawmakers Target Meatpacker Consolidation Amid High Prices
outside_the_pit.body: Sen. Schumer introduced legislation aimed at increasing competition in meatpacking, arguing consolidation among the nation's largest processors has contributed to high grocery prices. The bill comes as cattle prices hold steady while retail beef costs remain elevated, suggesting margin capture at the processing level.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Finalizes Visual-Only Swine Inspection Rule
outside_the_pit.body: FSIS transitioning to primarily visual postmortem inspection for swine instead of manual incision, streamlining plant operations. The change could reduce processing bottlenecks and labor costs, potentially improving packer margins in a tight-capacity environment.

outside_the_pit.title: Strategic Petroleum Reserve Ships Rare Asian Cargo
outside_the_pit.body: First U.S. oil shipment to Asia since late 2022 departed for Philippines as Hormuz crisis reshapes global trade flows. The rare SPR export signals Washington's confidence in domestic supply security even as Middle East tensions persist.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: Carry working as old-crop moves into commercial hands and new-crop pricing begins. The spread held its width even as July fell, telling you storage is getting paid and the calendar reflects real supply flow. Wider carry means less producer selling pressure on new-crop.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: **Illinois and Indiana elevators tightening bids** as ethanol plants return from maintenance turnarounds. Eastern Belt basis gaining 3-5 cents over futures as grind capacity comes back online. Western Belt staying soft on rail congestion, consistent with the seasonal pattern. Gulf export basis unchanged, reflecting steady but not urgent overseas demand.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex split resolve through feeder strength or live cattle weakness?
weekly_thread.status_text: The convergence held for a second day, live and feeder cattle both steady at narrow spreads. The question is shifting from which direction the split resolves to whether the convergence itself is sustainable.

the_more_you_know.title: The planting paradox: why early doesn't always mean better
the_more_you_know.body: Corn planted ahead of the 5-year average sounds bullish, but the calendar tells a more complex story. **Early planting in wet soil conditions can reduce stands** and delay emergence as much as late planting in ideal conditions. The Belt's 42% planted figure beats the average, but soil moisture levels are running 150% of normal in key districts. Prevent plant deadlines matter more than planting pace when May turns soggy. The market prices planting progress, but emergence and stand establishment drive yield potential.

USDA Crop Progress; corn above 45% confirms Belt acceleration
Memorial Day cash cattle trade reset after holiday

daily_quote.text: Nothing in this world can take the place of persistence.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA reports, energy markets, agricultural news services
